speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was recorded in Hershiel Tribble's workshop on Hatter Creek, Princeton in July 1973. Hershiel was born in 1896. His parents were among the first settlers in the Princeton area. They separated when Hershiel was young and he was raised by his mother. Hershiel worked as a scaler and clerk for Potlatch Forest in the Bovill area for many years. Early times around Princeton centering on his parents and logging work are main topics of this first conversation with Hershiel. The second side was recorded after lunch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The first thing I wondered was what you heard about things that your father did before you were born, I mean before you were around.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, he was a miner for a long time. He mined in the Hoodoo district there in the tributaries. Well, on Camas Creek, we did both plaster and quartz mining, and quartz on a small scale. He had an arastow wheel there that he ground out little quartz with for sampling and such as that. That was not far up the creek, only about a mile and a half, two miles up from the mouth of Camas Creek. And around in there in the Gold Hill Country, he did quite a lot of mining. And he also logged in the early day here at Princeton with Oxen, logged in with Princeton with Oxen. And he owned a large amount of property around here, the best off man financially of anybody, although he owed it quite a lot in 1993. He went broke and everything closed up, he couldn't get money or anything else. And he went broke and lost it on Batia. He did do a cat horse. He had quite a lot of these little Indian ponies and such as that. They didn't have much value, but they had quite a lot of experiences, I think, in the mining and such as that. And then he finally, in his latter days, he went over around Gifford and raised beans. He went and then about 40 acres of beans and harvested them. He had quite a lot of trouble with them because the weather so many times, you know. It would destroy them for them or damage them badly, keep them in sheds that couldn't get them dry enough to thrash. And he worked at that pretty much until he was retirement age, until he had to put it in. And then he moved to Moscow and lived there the last few years of his life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how he came to own so much land? How he started getting land?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, he came in and logged and worked hard. He was a hard worker all his life and he worked hard. And he speculated. That's how he'd come to get the land. That's also how he lost it. Because he would owe on it, see, and he was keeping up things and he would owe everything he bought while he'd owe someone. When he'd seen a chance to buy some real cheap, maybe he got a little hog, or he'd say, yes, and he'd buy more land. And then when the hard time struck, why, there was no work, there was no money, and he couldn't make payments on them, see, on the things that he owed. And he couldn't even sell part of it to save the rest of it. So it was kind of a rough time they had those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about what it was like before they made there be a town here? How it actually got settled up?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It seemed like your father had something to do with it if he owned quite a bit of land around here.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, yes, but there was nobody living on any of this land hardly at that time, see. It was just in the roar of the primitive state very near. Indians would come in, you know, and dig camels on this meadowlands there. Timothy, it seemed natural Timothy grew on those. And this camels was real thick. And there'd be several outfits of Indians, I can remember those myself quite well, that they used to camp there just south of Princeton, through the hundred yards, put up their teepees and dig this camels. Guess they made, they'd hammer out making bread, I guess, is what I always heard. I never stuck around eating anything. So they were still coming when you were a little boy. Oh, yes. My mother was still afraid of them. Now the wars was over, my father, he had taken part in some of the Indian wars, but while the wars was over and all that, but you know, even today we got outlaws and everything, and of course the Indians didn't actually suspicion them or if anything was dead white. It was destroyed or anything, they'd laid on the Indians, I guess the Indians would lay it on the white men. The women didn't feel too happy to be left alone or anything, and then usually my father tried to never leave my mother alone. She was a real good horseman. She got through one time and drug the foot in the stirrup quite a ways. And her health, she had very bad health and she had many operations during her lifetime. She always thought that it all dated back to this injury from being drug with a horse. I don't know that with a supposition, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that when she was a child?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No, no, no, that's when her and my father was living together. She married my father. She rode a horse and my father forbid her to ride, see, and he didn't figure it was safe for her, and he forbid her to ride it. One day he was gone, she was quite a horsewoman and she felt as though she could ride it. And so she got on it and it killed her and she didn't get one foot out of the stirrup, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what kind of injuries she got?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No, I don't know. Perhaps some internal injuries because she had several operations. Of course, one operation was for gold. I don't think they ever had any of that. But I really don't know too much about that. But even back to my memories, there was no settlement beyond about a mile south of Princeton, about as far as the settlement's reach, until the homestead was come in. And of course, my folks, a lot of them took up homesteads in this country. And that was of course before my time, but I can remember back to where these old homestead cabins were real good. I used to like to go in there as the kids would look around, you know, when they generally had wood ratchets in there we could shoot and things. They took over after the people with them. But you're saying that your father was here before the homesteaders came in. I think he was because quite a lot of my people homesteaded. I had an uncle that homesteaded the very place I live on here. The homestead's a mile strict here. I used to own, my brother and I owned a half a mile right in the middle of it. There was one forty on the south end and the north end that we didn't have. And we had the middle eighty out of this hundred sixty homesteaded. And so I'm quite sure that my father must have been here before the homestead by then. But as far as any business for people living outside of that, they just lived on what they had to do, you know, to prove up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he actually made a living mining when he was doing it?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Oh yeah, see he never made anything big. I never knew anybody getting rich out of it. Yet I guess there was probably several million dollars taken out of this country altogether, but there was probably hundreds of people mining. But they would get enough, you know, and finally get out of there. They were still getting gold and they quit mining. But they'd pan out maybe about two dollars a day, a few of them good. But when wages got up and things were, they couldn't live at that, then they would begin to quit mining. And another thing then, there would begin to be farms appear along the Puth River. And these farmers objected to placer mining. They'd throw silt down from the mines, you know. Some of it was hydraulic mining, some of it was plane digging and panning, using cradles. I had no cradle out here in the barn for years that my father made and had. I still have two gold pans. And they couldn't afford to pay these farmers damage, you see, for what little there was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I heard that your father, maybe this made me wrong, but I heard that he was mining, where was he mining?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, he mined mostly on the Camas Creek. That's between here and Harvard. Right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'd heard that he had taken over some of the water that Emmett Utt's father couldn't use because he'd been flooding out Jim Cochran's land.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yeah, well, that happened. Jim Cochran's land was flooded in the water. He was threatening his suit. And he lives right there, you see, on Camas Creek, in the flat lands there. And he was one of the ones that was causing trouble with the miners. And the miners, of course, they thought they should be able to mine. And the farmers, they didn't want the silt down on their meadows. So they kind of closed the miners out pretty much. They'd had to put up bombs, you know, and they couldn't afford to do it for what little they were getting out of it. And then it played out. It was slow. But, you know, later years, a good many years later, in fact not too often long, they put in a dredge in the same country up in here where the mines was. Because that was mostly a little further up on the Palouse. But they put in a dredge there and took out a lot of gold there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how your father got his claim?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, I don't know for sure now. I don't know if he homefed it or not. I don't think some mother homefed it up at, what's now, Laird Park and, well, they called it Grizzly Camp then. But now they called it the Grizzly Camp, I guess. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it's the other way around, right?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: They called it the Camp Grizzly. Yeah, that's what it is. I know they changed the name of it somehow. But she had a homestead there when I was six years old. And I think we left there just about that time or a little afterwards. It may come down to at least within a quarter of a mile of Camp Grizzly there. It was just a very short distance there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did she have to live on the place to prove it up?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: She did have to live there a while. And my oldest brother made a living for her. He worked out. And I stayed there with her when I was a little kid. And then I had a nephew, my sister's boy. He was only six. He was two years young when I was four years old. No, I'll take it going back. He was four years young when I was. He was two years old. And we stayed there with her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like in the place? Was it pretty primitive?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yes, it was real primitive.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: But there was just a homestead here and there. And the people only stayed on them. I forget what the requirements was, but they had to stay on them so long each year, you know. And we stayed in there. And they could come out to send children to school and for various things. So we didn't have to stay on them for a little while. But it was nice back in there. It was cool and shady and mushrooms, big old mushrooms. Half as big as your head. You can't even find any anymore. And in there, you know, there wasn't too much deer in the early days. I think there was more deer here in the last few years. They were pretty scarce this last year. But then there used to be. You didn't see many deer. You see some playing. A few cougar. I never saw a cougar myself. My father, I think, killed a cougar or two. And a bear, quite a few bear. But I think the more there is now. But you know, the woods was primitive. Everything was primitive. The streams were pure and clean, you know. They're not polluted like they are now. They were not molested. You could catch nice trout in any of those streams.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: It was a lot different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the farms really small?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, there actually wasn't any what you'd call farms in those days. They had Timothy, the most more natural crop on the flats that they would cut. And pasture, they were talking things. There was practically no farming did with machinery. Of course, machinery, they used horses those days. But they'd farm a little bit maybe and they'd have gardens and things. But there was nobody living there except for one once in a while along the river in the flat ground, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you talking now about when you were just a little boy?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yes, I am.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: We moved around quite a little. I lived in Palouse for a while. I went my first term of school in Palouse. And that was, I don't know, I didn't go to the start until I was about seven. There's an old log cabin that has been down for many, many years. Very few people even know that there existed there anymore. Just you went in town. But at that time there was only two or three automobiles as far back as I remember. About two I think in Palouse around that country and I think maybe three in the middle of Moscow. And one fellow there by the name of Half-Eyed, a blacksmith. He wasn't a Negro but he was sure black and he'd give the kids all rides on the motorcycle. He'd be a nice old guy. I wouldn't ride on that. I was always afraid to run. I never would ride on a motorcycle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Palouse like then?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, it was real lively. Before might, well, there was at one time there was supposed to have been thirteen saloons in that little town of Palouse. They had a mill. Now if you take to Pullman or anything you leave town and go with the rays there on the north hill we used to call it. And down on the river, on the Palouse river, there was a mill set right in there where the railroad tracks and the highway goes now. And that was one thing they kept to. Then they had to row houses uptown there. They called it the mill row. Houses all practically like built for the crews. They had quite a number of churches there. They had a street they called Church Street. I don't know. There must have been five or six different churches all along that street. Putting all the churches in Palouse was on that one street. And in town there I remember a fellow by the name of McLemire in a drug store for many years. He'd been dead for a long time now. And gone. We're in a hard raised boy. Still runs it. And I knew him quite well and the other boy quite well. But then they had the schools. Now when I went there they had the one school. They had this old school I said I first went to. They had a school on the north hill. The one on the south hill. And I went to almost every room in all the three of those schools. But we moved around. I was there when they put in this W on the railroad. They put in that cement wall there. It was in 1906. And this kid was playing on there. They had them old cans. You know. And powder cans. Black powder cans. Blasting. And there was generally a spoonful or so of powder left in the bottom of them. And I gathered up there with the other kid. And I said well I said I did with a bunch of us kids. And we'd take them out and set a pile of powder where you could get out of them. And then light them with a match and watch them flash. And I got the bright idea it took a flame to set it off. And I had a stick there. The fire owner went out and I got the blue on this red ember there. Getting down close to the powder. And we went to powder and I thought I'd lost my eyes. I couldn't see for a little bit. It was filled with ashes and dust. And seen as my eyebrows. And I said well I'm blind. It didn't really injure me any but it was a pretty close call.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember as a kid about the putting in of all this new work when they put in the railroad and the walls and the mill?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well my mother looked at that property. She was in the market. She sold it to this homestead up here. She sold it. And she was looking for a place she wanted to buy. And she looked at the property right exactly where it was on Potlatch stems. But that was full of little potholes there you know. And they just all around. Dozers now they wouldn't think anything about it but those days when they worked with horses. Well you didn't see how you could farm it or do anything near with it. So she decided not to buy it and she bought a place over north of Potlatch stems about two and a couple miles or so. And later on it filled it there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was nothing at Potlatch stems was there?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Oh no. Absolutely nothing. And then they come in after that you see and then bought this up and put in the town and the railroad and everything. That was real early in the day. I don't know what year that was in. But I wouldn't say they were unknown because when we lived on that place there north of Potlatch I went to school there see. So Potlatch must have been known there but they come in and started building the railroad in 1906. But the town of Potlatch said they hadn't established it yet I'm sure at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did a lot of people come in then when they started the building?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well yeah there was quite a lot worked on the railroad you know. They worked here and there they put crews in one place and some more up some other place you know. So they could have different crews working at the same time and quite a few come in at that time. And then they started they bought that land up there with the town a height and they started building the town. See that was a one-man town everything was for many many years you know it just belonged to Potlatch. The bank was put in there just to help the working men that worked at the mill was all it was for. And the store was they advertised everything to eat and work with but it was it was Potlatch store everything was there Potlatch you couldn't own Potlatch. It was house and Potlatch at that time. And so it all belonged to come though that put a lot of people in there to work to see to operate all these things and work the railroad they got started. And of course in the railroad scene why there was various other things to build in. But speaking about the early days there now there was a pretty busy place there in Palouse at that time with all those saloons and there was a brewery as you went in. There just at as you're going to pray in Palouse from the east side right on Main Street. There was no thing there wasn't well nowadays they wouldn't allow it in a hundred miles of civilization I don't think. Not sure perfume the town smell up the windows right anyplace.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What it smell like? Did it stink or did it smell like Olympia does now?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Oh it smelled I guess like. Well of course they go through a brewery now then there's not a bad odor in them at all now. But I don't know hardly it's a fermenting you see the brew there. I think that went in large amounts you know that's a well I think it's open kettles for a while I think and a lot of perfume goes off from the office. So Princeton was just tiny compared to Palouse. Oh yes Princeton was just tiny actually Princeton started there west on what they call Hampton. That was the original Princeton I think it was anyway that's where the store and post office was I can remember back to that. That store and post office there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know why it moved?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: I don't exactly know I tell you well yes I think maybe I do I think that when the pot lights come in I think they put the depot that was up a ways on the hill from the railroad track. And Princeton was right on the level and they would be and they put the depot there see. And there's people lived around too like my father over there logging there's other people were you know logging too and things but there was no town. I don't know if there's even a store there at this time that was what I'm talking about now is when I was a little too young you see. I just can remember being up there I can remember I had a scarlet fever when I was four years older and I was quarantined for a long time and I couldn't revive it. And then by the name of Turner we were on the store and post office. My grandfather he had a house there and he had a dance hall there. He used to go to that to have all the entertainment for the dances at this old dance hall. I remember he got leaning and he had three poles on the west side of leaning toward the west. And he had three big poles that looked like telephone poles up there for braces to keep upsetting. He was getting so good and I probably wasn't too strong to start I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now how about how Princeton got its name?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: It was named after a horse my mother had. They tried and it was going to name it after her and her name was Coronetti. And they decided to name the town of Cora. And they sent this in to wherever they send to get it registered I don't know county, state or capital. And they couldn't name it that because there was already a Cora in Idaho. And so then they decided to name it after a horse by the name of Prince. I believe it was a Pinto it was a spotted pony some kind of mother's. And they decided to name the town after this spotted horse and there was also a town by the name of Prince. So they called it named Princeton. So it was actually named after a horse that my mother had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know anything about what the Depression did in 1893 to this country?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well people didn't have any money actually. But what it did they couldn't get any money then. They just tightened down they couldn't get any money and then there's some of these land companies come in. I would hate to name them in the background of my mind. I think there's one called Hypotheque or something. Hypotheque, yeah. Pronounced Hypotheque. Yes that's one of the outfits that had it. And then there was a fellow around here by the name of Cod, Bill Cod. That bought up these timber my mother I think sold him I believe it was. And he bought up all this timber then at the time. I believe the potlice I'm not sure about this now. I believe the potlice bought some of this stuff from some of the other companies from the smaller companies I don't know. But some of these big outfits you see they had mortgages on all this. So when times got rough they called in for clothes. Of course I guess they were mostly after the property rather than the money. And the fellas owed them why they couldn't pay it. That's what wrecked my father and I guess it wrecked other people and they had to start all over again. But they had no money to pay you see. They had quite a lot of time on them normally. Some payments but they just closed down and they had to have it right then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where else? Do you think that many people left at that time?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No I don't think they left. I don't think they left at all. Those people are in here. Most of them I think liked the country and everything. It was a new country and they didn't have any money to go anyplace else. They didn't have any place else to go really. Where they could better themselves I don't think hardly anybody left here at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you think they started to come out of it?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well I think things pretty soon began to develop a little more. They began to do any way they could. Maybe that little miller somebody did have a little private property left or something and saw a little number for somebody. They traded back and forth mostly without money. Some fella if he wanted lumber maybe traded somebody a cow or some root of Vegas or something else. Started to barter and trade around for things. Everybody helped one another if anybody burned out or had hard luck. Everybody just grabbed the tools and everything. They went right over there and they built him a new house because they would make them out of logs. Grab the logs and they didn't cost them anything. Start him up again that way in a home. They lived on almost nothing. They raised calves, have cows, you know, a few cows and milk and butter and things that way. And then they'd have pigs, you know, they'd raise their own lard and pork. And things that way they gradually began to get a little on the farming idea. What little they could. And different things. Trade work. They just worked without money almost.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would they do if they didn't have any land though?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, once in a while they'd find somebody big enough that needed help or something that would maybe give a little bit of money. Just a tiny bit, you know, for things they had to have. Maybe he'd pay them and something. If they was that hard up, why they'd need things too. Maybe divide a plan with them or something to get them to work if they didn't have enough to pay them. Anyway to get by those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear anything from your father about that times?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No, I didn't. My mother, I don't know when father, my mother didn't. After she left my father she didn't want me to have anything to do with him or see him or anything, which was all wrong. But she thought that he would want to get me back, kidnap me or something. He probably didn't even want me. But no, he was a nice guy, a nice actor. I did get acquainted. I didn't really get acquainted with him the way he was. Until I was almost grown and I found out that he was a real nice man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear anything from her about it? About these times? The depression?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Not too much. I don't think she paid too much attention to them. She generally felt a little better. I don't mean that she had money, but a woman got along better than a man did those days. She took up this claim, you know, and she got a real high price on it. She had a real nice claim up there. She got $2300, which was a lot of money at that time. She could have got a lot more money than that, which she had held off. But she got that and then she bought this place I spoke about out at North of Patlihatch. I don't know if she sold that. She got an idea about going to Cuesta, Spokane, and bought five acres up there. I went to school one term there. She was going to make it on a little truck farm, which was out of the question. She couldn't make anything. Everybody else around Spokane was trying to make a living that way. She soon got out of there and then she kept powers from her grandfather. They were in Palouse a while. I took care of him until he died. The last years of his life, that's how I came to go to school in Palouse while we were there. I remember when a little kid, when you got a pension from the Civil War of $12 a month. It was pretty hard scraping around, but he had two houses and lots there and he kept one rented all the time. He kept bees and he had horses and he kept gardening and everything. And Mother, she didn't pay Mother anything. She was her father and she was glad to see her there. But she just provided the groceries and said, oh, he might have given her a little bit. She knows where Mike goes. I don't know about that, but I think maybe she still had a little bit of money. But it was pretty rough going there. And then that pension, I remember the last I knew, most he ever got was the double of $24 a month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, about your father, did you, do you know any stories about him that can sort of tell what kind of guy he was? Or what different things that he did and that you heard about him?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, he was full of fun. Real full of fun. And always joking about everything. He was a happy-go-lucky guy. He used his politics to joke with a lot. And you like me to mention that, what I mentioned the other day when I was seeing you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah, that's a good...

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, yeah, a few little incidents. One time he or he and some of the others, during the mining, someone was killed. I don't know whether my father killed or who, but one of them killed a cougar. And up between Princeton and Harvard, kind of on the hillside above the old road there, they propped it up among the rocks and things so it looked like it was alive. And there was some Chinese men working on the mines up there, coming along. About scared them to death. They went way out around and everybody would come along for a while. They had a lot of fun out of that. They thought they probably did. And another little incident I remember, he was always just joking and cutting up all the time. But anyway, a little incident I remember, him and an old fellow, he was a distant revelation of ours, but he wasn't very well liked by anybody, including us. But I see us sometimes before my time, or not before my brothers and brothers remember it, but anyway. He met this old fellow. He was an old fellow in a wagon going down the road and I don't know, they got into some kind of an argument. It could have been political, they were on different size events. I don't know what it was, but anyway, this old fellow, he had a .22 rifle in the wagon. He picked this up and pointed at my father and threatened to shoot me. My father told him, he says, if you ever shoot me at that and I find it out, he says, I'll beat the hell out of you.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: He was that kind of a man. He wasn't afraid of anything. I think I'd have run if it had been me. I'm not going to burn for me. I've used too many of myself and killed too many off in my time. I know what they can do. But little things, he was always little things that way. My nephew and I, he was kind of like bees, he usually had a few bees. He was fooling around with his bees and my nephew, he was afraid of bees, real afraid of them. My father says, oh they don't want to sting you if you just keep back and leave them alone. He said they won't sting you. So my nephew kept getting a little closer and my father says, well, one of them is dinging, but if you get too close. He kept getting up there and he thought my father didn't get stung right among them, he could get among them and pretty soon one stung him. My father says, I told you, I knew that, he says, I knew it, he says, they'll sting a Republican every time. He was a radical Republican, my mother was a radical Democrat and I didn't know what to be. But he was always pulling jokes about that and he'd tell anybody if there was a Republican, if they was hollering about something, he'd say what they voted for.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: He sure made a lot of noise. You'd think that people would want to shoot him, but actually he was always a joke and did it in fun. He'd get by with it where other people couldn't probably. But he was awfully well liked, all my folks that taught the world and all, my father.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he very political himself?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, he was county commissioner here in Moscow for I don't know how long, but he represented this district for a while. I think he's central committeeman and he didn't take any very big active part or try to run for it. I think that's the highest of them and I don't think he cared too much about going up into politics. I don't think anybody did. I think the Democrats were alright.

speaker: Unidentified Speaker
words: How's she going?

speaker: Unidentified Speaker
words: Oh fine. Now you guys haven't done a darn thing I bet you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean? We've been sitting here talking?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh that doesn't matter. I'm sorry. It doesn't matter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you my wife's get-to-know? Are you about ready to come in and have a bite with me?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Oh, we could have a beer first. We'll come in and have your beer and then you can have your dinner. Okay. Fine.

speaker: Unidentified Speaker
words: I don't know if we've got any place now but we sure had a lot of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get into the war?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: There really wasn't many Chinese around. The three or four of them had a mine that others had passed up along up on the Camas Creek a ways, a mile and a half, two miles up from the mouth. They didn't associate much with other people or vice versa. And they began to get to the head of the miners probably that these Chinese were making a lot of money which nobody knows for sure. So they robbed them and killed them and nobody ever knew who did it. And in those days they didn't try very hard to find out, especially the word was Chinese. And nobody ever knew if they got any money. Lots of people don't think they got any money really out of it. But there wasn't too many foreigners around. Mostly they were local white people that mined different nationalities naturally. But very few foreigners.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to the Chinese?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, of course they killed those and I don't know what they did. I don't know if people buried the bodies or what. But he never heard. That was a little before my time so I just have to know what my father and my older brothers told me about it. But this is on the same creek that my father mined on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He never had any idea who did it?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No. Not that I know of. Probably. Maybe some people did know who did it. But might not have been interested in saying anything or having any part in it. That's the way those things happened lots of times, especially in the early days. Nobody around near it to report it to and it had to come by horseback to get in there and investigate it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it would have been much different if it was white guys that had been killed instead of Chinese?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yes, I think it would have been some more different but still there were things come up that I've heard them tell about that I don't remember the incidents or anything. Or there was murders committed and they paid very little attention to it. Of course in those days if you just get across the line into Canada, that was all there was to it. Nobody tried to follow him any further and lots of people didn't even do that. They just moved around some other location. There wasn't much law and order those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know any other stories about violence or murders, around?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, my memory isn't very good. There was a murder committed right here in a little over a quarter of a mile from my place. There was some jealousy, something over a woman further up the line here up the creek. He came down and shot a man off my roof who worked in but he skedaddled up to Canada. That was a story anyway and nobody ever got a hold of him. Nothing ever did about it. I guess they both wanted to go to the same girl or something that way. I don't know just what they said it was. But personally, I don't know. By the time I was old enough to know too much about these things, the law and order was getting a lot better established. They began to get more roads and even got the railroad in when I was fairly young. And then of course with that came telegraph wires and telephones. So crime wasn't so easy and people began to work for companies and get a paycheck rather than mining and mining didn't any longer pay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are some of the different jobs that your mother did? What was it like for a woman making a living?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: She was pretty hard up. She kept eating in on the money she had from her timber claim. But she did keep house a little bit. In those days, taking in washing would keep house for somebody or something. Her and I lived at a farm. She kept house and did the housework for a man for a while. And she wasn't able to take in washing. And she worked a little bit picking fruit and various things. Her and I went up to Spokane when I was a kid and we picked cherries and strawberries and cucumbers, whatever we could, and things like that. And my mother made an attempt with other parties at the time or two to run a restaurant in Spokane but they failed. She was a good cook but she wasn't a businesswoman when it came to anything like that. She didn't have any regular profession so it was kind of hard for her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me that she ran the mail?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yes, she did run the mail. That was before my time, before my memory. I was real small or before I was born. I'm not sure. She did run a hack and a team of horses from what is known as Hampton or part of Princeton now. There's a store and post office combination there and she run a mail and even carried passengers from there up to Grisel Camp for a while. My sister even took her place a little while when she was sick or something. I don't know just how long, I don't think it was too great of a time but she did run this mail horse in the days of the modern horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your brother?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your brother who was a cook in a camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What sort of jobs did he do?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, he never exactly learned a profession. I mean he never studied one but he had three professions that he came pretty good at through his lifetime. One, he was a pretty good butcher. He did the butchering at the town of Bovill for the meat market that supplied all the Potlatch number of company camps and the town of Bovill. At one time he did all their butchering and he stayed with that a while. He didn't like it too well but he worked at anything he could to make a living. He and his wife and the girls lived up there and I stayed with him and graduated in eighth grade in school there while I was staying with them at Bovill. And then later on he became a cook in the potlatch camps and he was cooked for quite a while. Several years he spent cooking and he gained a name of being a good cook. He was very clean and a cook and everybody seemed to like him pretty well. But then in later years when the logging kind of played out he went to carton to work. Ricky had always been pretty handy with tools and he went to work building houses and repairing and what not. I worked there with him for about three years there at carton to work. He helped build several houses around this community. And then up at Deary he built one and Avon one. And he spent most of his time the latter years of his life working at carton to work until he got too old to work and retired.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What of your sister?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: My sister, she married a railroad man. They lived in Spokane for quite a while and he was in charge of the pumps on the SP &S. He used to take out the extra gang there to work sometimes. He was a pretty high official in the railroad system. He kept the pumps and repair all along the line and supplied the trains with water. They lived there until he was transferred to Portland and that's where they lived until my sister died.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: My other brother, Arvid, between Ellen and I, he worked at first one place and then another. He worked at Chico, California for a farmer grafting and budding on shrubbery and trees for a while. And he worked in the seedy yard at Bovill for a while. But he settled down working in one of these supply stations that my brother-in-law was in charge of up by Sprague, Washington. He supplied the, well the town where he lived in was Lamont and that's where the supply station was. He operated the pumps and stuff there and the roiling and various things that they supplied the trains with. And he lived there until his retirement and moved to Spokane and he and his wife and he died in Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, let's talk about your early years in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you, how did you come to start working in the woods?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, I started in, my brother and I, we bought, well my brother had first had 40 acres of land here on the Hatter Creek near Princeton, south of Princeton. He sold it and we together bought 80 acres from the Potlatch lumber company and received a contract to log it off. We bought a river, Palouse River at Princeton any way that we could which was usually what they called go devils, a couple of runners with a bunk across one end, the log set up on that and the other end drug the ground and fastened with chains. Horses of course is what we used for power. And so we spent about three winters logging this while I was too young to hire out for companies. And then we drove it to Potlatch with a spring and the high water came up and ice break up and the logs released. So we drove what was left up in the river and drove it to Potlatch. And then after that I went to work in the woods camp up around Bovill and I worked at anything I could get for a little while there and they were having trouble. I bucked wood for ground line donkeys for a while and I run hoisting engines, loading engines on the donkeys for loading cars a little bit. Then I became a vacancy in the clerk and scaler. One man had to do the clerk and scaler for the all crew, sometimes a hundred men. And they'd been having trouble getting clerks and scalers. They didn't know the scalers, pretty hard job, they kept them so busy. So they gave me that job, I didn't know anything about it. So several of them helped me out and I managed to hold it until I learned it. Some nights I'd have to scale all day during the day and then some nights at the end of the month I'd have to set up maybe twelve, one o 'clock for 30 nights making out the payroll and writing out the checks. It was very difficult for me until I got onto it and then finally it got so much easier and I could do it. But then eventually while they changed the system, at that time when I scaled all those in the woods all I had to do was just mark them anyway that I could tell them. There was no numbering or anything and just numbered them on the landing. But then they got to putting more work on there and so then they divided the scaling and the clerking and they'd have a clerk and a scaler in the camp. The clerk would keep the books then and I didn't like the clerking myself so I kept getting out of it all I could and so I took over gradually the scaling. I scaled the logs, I was a landing scaler and they kept increasing until finally they had a scaler in the woods for the saws and a landing scaler and a scaler on the marin. When they'd get to paying everybody with a thousand, I worked at that for several years and I worked at that until they began to shut down during the winters.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: And always before they had run steady but they started in so they just logged through the summer in good weather and then I worked. I was only getting about three months, three or four months of work a year and I couldn't make a living for a family at that. I scaled the, an old section scaler in Iowa, they scaled the first logs that was hauled up a truck to Potlatch. So I had to turn that down because I couldn't get steady enough work and then I worked with my brother at Cartner, worked for about three years. It became an opening for a mail carrier at Princeton. There was about eighteen of us supplied and I took the exam and it happened to win it. I was lucky I guess and it was only a tri-weekly route at that time only three trips a week and very little money in it. It's a little that I built two trailer houses in my yard for people to help make a little more money. And then we also run a few cows and milk and our kids was getting big enough by that time then to milk cows. We sold a little cream and one thing or another until the mail route got eventually longer and longer until it got up enough so that I could make a living at it. And I carried mail then for twenty-five years until I retired in 1960. Since then I haven't did much work, my health hasn't been good enough to hold down a job and I don't do any hard work. You do a lot of wood scraps work. Well, I have a pretty nice little woodworking shop. I have probably one of the best woodworking shops around. Quite a lot of machinery and tools and I do work a little repair work and I can have some wonderful grandchildren. They came in between the top of the bridge near for me and they put a metal roof on the garage, a real large garage, and a three car size and size for shop. They put that on for me. I furnished the material and them and their wives came and they took wire scratched bushes and scratched the old paint off of my shop and off of my house and repainted it all. Well, they did all these things for nothing. Wouldn't take anything for it. So I wanted to do something for them when the boys wanted to help him make a gun cabinet. But he wasn't busy and didn't get around to it so I made the gun cabinet for him. Then I started in and I made a gun cabinet for each of the boys. I made one out of just the front glass doors and the front was some drawers and everything were made out of white pine. Then the next one I made one of a naughty white pine in the front and the last one made out of bird's eye pine. And they furnished just the material and I did the work. It was quite a long, tedious job making them. And I let them do the finishing of the painting and such as that. And I made window boxes for the granddaughters and such. I made a water wheel for my daughter and a windmill for my wife. So I spent my spare time working shop things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I want to get back to the scaling in the woods that you started talking about and didn't describe how it was done then when they were first scaling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was it that you did scale the logs there when you first started?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Yes, well when I first started I didn't scale them in the woods for the sawyers. The sawyers worked by the day and the logs were only scaled once. And that was a little later. I was a little ahead of my stories later when they got to Chipwing. I scaled them for the sawyers and I only had to mark them anyway to tell. But at first you would scale them on the landing and then I clerked which was a very big job because they had a hundred men in that camp and they were coming and going all the time. But later then they eased that situation up. But the scaling of the logs, of course they used what they call a decimal C. There's several others. There's a scripter and a doyl. One thing another, they used what they call a decimal C that gives one-tenth of the scale. You have to add a cipher onto it to get the full scale. And you measure the distance inside the bark. One side of a log the other on an average. You get the average size and figure the length of it in your room. I'll tell you then for that length, about that size log, the number of board feet is supposed to saw out. And then you have to mark that on cards and carry it in your books and all the way through and add that all up. And the month make out reports for how many board feet of logs they get out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say that you could scale it any way, what does that mean?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Average way. Average way. I think probably misunderstood that. The average way. You know sometimes they're round and sometimes they're sort of oval. And if you scaled a narrow way at the end of the log, it wouldn't be a fair scale. Or if you scaled a wide way of it, it wouldn't be. So you take the average diameter, see, of the small end of the log and measure it in inches. And well you just look at the scale, it tells you on the rule, see, how much that log will give. You have three different columns, it only gives you how much of it's a 12 foot log, if it's a 14, a 16, an 18, or a 20. All those figures are on the rule in a row for each one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this change as time went on?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: No, no it didn't. I don't, I believe now, to this day I believe they still use the same decimal rule. I don't know of course the late years, but I think they still use the decimal rule.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could one guy be expected to scale and clerk at the same time?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Scaling seems like a full time job. It was a full time job, that's right it was. It didn't, it had been easy to scale the logs in a day at that time, quite easily. Because most of the logs were long logs, 32 feet, see. Your scale rule doesn't give a 32 foot log, but you do. You take the two 16s, figures of two 16s, and you figure an average gain of one inch, so we figure the second log. In most cases, that won't apply to all kinds of timber, in most cases we'd figure the next inch, put that and add the two of them together to find the scale of that 32 foot log. So that is fairly fast, see. A ground line donkey, doing their best, would put out about five or six carloads of these logs. And that wasn't too hard for one scaler, but they'd be a donkey up on one spur, that was the railroad track, short railroad track, they'd run up in there, and maybe another, and off down a mile or two or three, and some other place. You had quite a little walking to do on that, and then you had to scale them on cards, and then when you got in camp you had to copy them in books, regular scale books. And then at the end of the month, well you kept your time, of course, which wasn't hard, at the end of the month then you had to make out payroll and write all the checks to these men along with that. Well I had to do that night, see. It really was too much, and they paid a clerk and scaler by the month, not by the day. So they didn't have any, you didn't get any overtime those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you learn how to clerk? How did they get us, what did you need to know and how did they break you in?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, that was kind of a stranger. They had been awful short of clerks and scalers, they couldn't have, because the foremen wanted me to take over, they'd been having trouble with clerks. They'd get one broke in there and supposed to be clerks, and they'd quit or something, and they'd had all kinds of trouble, and that was really bad, changing clerks and scalers all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was getting so hard that nobody wanted to scale for that camp, well, me, I had no profession of my own, and it was quite a thing for me if I could just hold that job down, and the foreman wanted me to because he knew me real well and I was a steady worker, and he thought maybe if I had it, why did I keep it?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: So he gave me a big lingo, he said that I'd never been in the office there, only to buy my tobacco, I smoked, and I'd never been there, that's all I knew about office work. And he told me that he used to be a contractor, he was, he had run gangs of men building railroads, but he didn't tell me that he didn't have any education and couldn't hardly sign his own name, he didn't tell me that, he told me he'd help me with the payroll. Then he told me that the auditor in Bowell, that would correct my mistakes, or he'd ask me if I made them, would help me, he'd learn me a lot about handling the clerical work. And the truth of the matter was, the little guy in Bowell, I'll not mention his name, he's a good little guy, and he was fine, he wasn't cranking it, but he never explained a thing. He'd just show me where I made mistakes and tell me how it should have been done or what should have been or something, and I didn't learn very much from him. Another man was supposed to show me how to make out the scale reports, and he was pretty good, he helped. But by the time I scaled all day and worked at night, he was just bad, I didn't know if I could possibly handle it or not. But the foreman, he didn't really have the right to hire a clerical scaler, that come under the superintendent at that time, and he told me he thought that if I could make it, and he'd talk to the superintendent, he could get him to keep me maybe, and they said unless they already had somebody hired. So it turned out they didn't already have somebody hired, and the superintendent agreed that I could stay. And so I stayed, and the work gradually got easier for me all the time, as I learned, I was always pretty good in arithmetic, and on making the reports and adding payrolls and things, I was pretty fast at it, and it didn't bother me very much after I had a little practice. I made quite a few mistakes at first, but I'd get so eventually that I'd hardly make a mistake, and carried 30 names on each sheet of the payroll, and maybe they'd change rates of pay by the hour, you know, 30 times on that, and you'd have to figure out how many hours at this rate, and how many hours at the other rate. Then they charged them a dollar a month hospital ticket, and they had the, one, they called a little store that I had operated to, where they sold stockings and shirts and pants, and sold printer everything that they used in camp, and charged that up to them, and keep it, and then if there's any deductions for contributions for things, I'd keep that all up and deduct it, but it wasn't too bad there, and finally as the camp got so and they required more, they began to have the woods, the timber sowed by the thousand, gypone lots of times they call it, and there had to be a scaler in the woods, and at first I took on some of that, and I just marked the logs any way I could, just to show that they were scaled, that's all I had to do. Now that was a big advantage, because sometimes I follow a tree up on a steep hill, and it slides clear to the bottom, and if you have to climb that mountain just to put a number on that stump, after you scaled it, that's quite a deal, see, so they went on that way for quite a while, and then all at once, we get orders, we had to number those logs, so we could be checked on, and so then, I'd have to number the tree, and then I'd have to go number the stump, and that caused a lot more work and trouble, and I come to find out the reason for this, that up at Elk River, there'd been two young scalers up there, there's supposed to be 6 ,000 feet of logs in the woods, and when they got them in, there was only 3 ,000. The sawyers had been paying them a little bit to slip them some extra scales, so that point of importance, the rest of us, all they did was fire them two boys, but the rest of us stayed there, we had to pay for it, of course then we had to start numbering ours and climbing the mountain to put the numbers on, so that's what we got for that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the difference if you numbered it up the mountain or if you numbered it down in the landing?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well, they had to correspond, you number the tree down then, and they'd go up and give the stump the same number to show that it'd come from that stump.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who checked? Who checked?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Oh, they had regular check scalers, full of the name of Jack Donovan checked most of the time I was there, and then there was a government man, Ed Keltie, he was using real nice for him, I was scared to death the first time he checked on me. I just thought, boy, I didn't know much about it, and I was trying to learn, and I was just there shivering while I was waiting for him to check, and he came back and he didn't see much. I told him I'm new, I said I don't know, I'd be glad to learn anything you can, I said I don't know anything about this. He said you've done alright, he said, and he went at it real nice, he says, there's a log or two out there, he says I just wanted, he says, what you'd give that, he says. Now he says sometimes opinions vary, you can't know too many, there'd be just a like on them, and maybe you know, and I'd look at one of them and say well, that's about what I've done, and another one, I'd give that a little bit more than you did, or I'd give it a little less than you did or something, see, and he'd say well now you made me just near right as I am, and he was just one of the nicest guys that I ever met, and he was always that way, just like that, and he'd tell you so nice that you didn't feel like you'd been reprimanded at all for it, you know, and I learned to really like him, and Donovan was a nice fellow too, he was the regular checkman, but he'd come out, oh I don't know how to tell you how often, and he'd come out and never, ten days maybe or so, he made the grounds at the camps, you see, but he and I, we always got along good, they're the ones that checked you, they was really our boss when they were on the scaling, but on the clerical work, why the auditor really at the town was our boss over that, but then finally when they got the sawyers working by the thousand there, the gypo and what they called it, the contract and however you want to, then they had to have a special scaler for scaling in the woods, they weren't too particular with that, it didn't have to match the landing scale, but they wanted to get a little accurate, they didn't deduct or rot or anything to start with, and then later on they did, but as they began to put more and more on, I took more scaler, so they had a scaler in the woods, and then I was a landing scaler, my scaler went back east to the warehouse, we scaled back to Minnesota, the results we had, the total of it, and then they had one on the marion just to go on to pay the marion crew for the thousand, the three scalers were almost wearing the end of the log drop, I guess it was all done, but I worked at the scaling then for quite a number of years

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: they went through some cycles when I first went to camp, no horses, they had what they call a ground donkey, they'd go out sometimes half a mile with them lines, maybe longer, but that was considered a pretty long haul, half a mile, maybe three thousand feet, that would be a little over half a mile, but they brought in the logs and the whole tree with those, to the landing, and then they'd have landing sawyers, this donkey would haul that in, you'd see a steam donkey, and they'd have landing sawyers, and there's another little engine on that operated on the same boiler as the big donkey did on that same platform, and they would load, the line would go up through a block on a pole, and go down in a crotch unit with a hook on each end of this crotch, and a man would take charge of each hook, and each one would take the hook and the end of the log, the same log, see what I mean, and one end, the other man would stick it in the other, and then the hoisting engine would hoist this end of this log up, and as it swung high enough to swing across the car, they'd drop it in the notches on the car, that's how they loaded it, and these hooks had ropes on them, so they had to jerk them and pull the hooks right back to them, and that's the way they loaded, and that's the way it was operated when I first went to camp, but then they brought in what they called the high lines, and those they had to slice down, they'd slice a wide strip for these high lines, the others they didn't, and they'd slice quite a wide strip because they'd knock the trees down a little bit, and they'd bring them in on that, and they'd be dangling in the air, now a lot of people didn't know, they worked a whole lot like the old fashioned K-carriers in the barns, and the tracks and stuff, it worked similar to those, although on those the hay goes clear up to the car and locks there, and then goes back in the barn, whereas with the high lines, as they call it, they don't never go clear up, very seldom go clear up the logs, they'll dangle, the ends of the chokers, the cables around them, the fasteners, will go up in the air high enough that it takes them over windfalls and holes and everything, but the other end of them usually touches the ground, it'll bounce along, if they go across a little draw, why even the other end will leave sometimes, but those are cut into logs, short logs, they're not cut for tree lengths, they bring them in, and then they got to using horses back there to drag some of them down to where the side line could reach them, and they can't reach out like they do in the chainways, they only change lines.

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: And they logged that way quite a while, and they have some high lines, maybe some ground line donkeys in the camp, then they had some small rigs, slides they call them I believe, that had a small end, they looked like a donkey engine, an upright engine on them, and they'd have a short line, I don't remember how far out they'd go, they generally wouldn't go over, I don't think, oh they could go out quite a way, but I did load most of them just around 100 or 2 feet from the track, and they could put them on pretty cheap that way because it didn't take them a minute to do it, you see, and not the big outfits, you know that's too close for them to work on them anyway, they used that method, and then they did use caterpillars for a while, they had one camp up here not far from where I live, that was all caterpillars, I scaled for that too when I was scaling, that was not the same time the others of course were bold, but I scaled right down here toward the home, and they began logging this part of the country, and then a little later, they went on with the cats and horses, they got into horses then, horses skidding short logs, and they hauled them in on what they called a bummer, it was like if you saw two blocks off a fairly large round log, and they'd drill a hole through the center, and put them on an axle, these two big blocks, they'd use them for wheels, and then they'd have a bunk across, they'd call their timber reach from one to the other, and they'd put these logs in them up on them just like they did the gold devils on when they were logging on snow, and chain them there and their back ends drag on the ground, and that's what they hauled in, they were a funny looking thing, pretty clumsy, but that's the way they'd upload most of their logging unless they were right close, then they might just get in the tongs, and then log with that for a long time, they did that pretty much, well I started seeing them until I retired, not quite, because they got to going into the trucks then finally, like a log today, and they got to using them quite a little bit, and they found out they didn't have to run this track in, they don't have to go with the donkey and all those things, and they can let the contract out to the men, let them worry about their trucks and everything, and so they started logging with them, and this man killed here, I said was a cheque scaler, and he and I, we scaled down here at the Potlatch Dam for the first summer that they logged with these trucks, but then I quit because I could no longer get work only just in the summer, that's the only time they could use those outfits, and they'd shut down things and I couldn't work, and then they began going into the trucks, and I started in, I worked a carton of work for a little bit until the mail route opened up, so that was the last I did the logging, but now then of course it's all done with big logging trucks every place, they haul them for miles and miles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You scaled and you clerked for one camp, right?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: Well I clerked for a different time, one that time, well now I scaled for two camps down here at home, because while there I had one camp, and then, well I was part of it, there's two of us scalers there at that time, and then I scaled for this caterpillar camp over here too, for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your family living down here while you were working up there?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: My family always lived down here all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you just got back down to see them occasionally?

speaker: Hershiel Tribble
words: That's right, I didn't get down very often now, I'll tell you. I got down, well my brother-in-law worked up there, he had a car and I didn't, we usually come down on weekends, we'd come down, we didn't have very long weekends, you know we'd have to.
